perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this Agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information from the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause regarding securities laws has been moved out of the 'Securities Laws' section and removed. The relocation diminishes the emphasis on insider trading restrictions, potentially misleading parties about their obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the purchase or sale of a security of any issuer, on the basis of material nonpublic information about that security or issuer, in breach of a duty of trust or confidence that is owed directly, indirectly, or derivatively, to the issuer of that security or the shareholders of that issuer, or to any other person who is the source of the material nonpublic information. Moving this clause from a specific section on Securities Laws to a vague statement in the document obscures its relevance and contradicts the need for clear disclosure.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes a clause explicitly addressing securities laws and insider trading restrictions. This contradicts the principle established in 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original text highlights the awareness of restrictions imposed by United States securities laws, while the changed text omits this crucial warning, potentially misleading parties about their obligations and undermining the legal requirement for clear disclosure and adherence to securities regulations.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and agrees that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the other party or any of its Representatives and that the non-breaching party would be entitled to seek equitable relief, including an injunction and/or specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and agrees that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the other party or any of its Representatives and that the non-breaching party would be entitled to seek equitable relief, only if agreed upon by both parties, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.explanation: The clause regarding injunctive relief is modified to say that equitable relief is only available if agreed upon by both parties. This changes the inherent right to seek an injunction into a negotiable term. Injunctive relief is a remedy that Delaware law makes readily available, so making it an optional provision makes it harder for the injured party to seek.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law allows for equitable remedies such as injunctions for breaches of contract. By requiring mutual agreement for injunctive relief, the modified text contradicts Delaware law's provision for such remedies, making it more difficult for the non-breaching party to obtain an injunction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 8 Authenticated PDF Â§ 8-401 Â§ 8-402 Â§ 8-403 Â§ 8-404 Â§ 8-405 Â§ 8-406 Â§ 8-407 TITLE 6 Commerce and Trade SUBTITLE I Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 8. Investment Securities Part 4 Registration Â§ 8-401. Duty of issuer to register transfer. (a) If a certificated security in registered form is presented to an issuer with a request to register transfer or an instruction is presented to an issuer with a request to register transfer of an uncertificated security, the issuer shall register the transfer as requested if: (1) under the terms of the security the person seeking registration of transfer is eligible to have the security registered in its name; (2) the endorsement or instruction is made by the appropriate person or by an agent who has actual authority to act on behalf of the appropriate person; (3) reasonable assurance is given that the endorsement or instruction is genuine and authorized (Section 8-402); (4) any applicable law relating to the collection of taxes has been complied with; (5) the transfer does not violate any restriction on transfer imposed by the issuer in accordance with Section 8-204; (6) a demand that the issuer not register transfer has not become effective under Section 8-403, or the issuer has complied with Section 8-403(b) but no legal process or indemnity bond is obtained as provided in Section 8-403(d); and (7) the transfer is in fact rightful or is to a protected purchaser. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original contract by requiring mutual agreement for equitable relief, including injunctions. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law allows for equitable remedies such as injunctions for breaches of contract. By requiring mutual agreement, the modified text makes it more difficult for the non-breaching party to obtain an injunction, contradicting the provision of equitable remedies available under Delaware law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly discuss equitable relief, the Perturbation and Law explanations focus on the contradiction of the general availability of injunctions, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware that are applicable to agreements made and to be performed within such State and without regard to its conflicts of laws rules or principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California that are applicable to agreements made and to be performed within such State and without regard to its conflicts of laws rules or principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Delaware to California could have a significant impact on how the contract is interpreted and enforced. This is particularly pertinent as this confidentiality agreement may be related to a possible acquisition. The change to California law may cause undue difficulty for the parties involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 provides that parties' choice of law will usually be respected, but not if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice, or if application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. Switching from Delaware (where EMC is likely incorporated) to California introduces a potential conflict, if neither party has a substantial relationship to California. EMC Corporation is located in Massachusetts, Isilon Systems is located in Washington.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Delaware to California introduces a potential legal contradiction, as highlighted by the Law Explanation referencing Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. If neither party has a substantial relationship to California, this change could be deemed invalid, especially if it contravenes a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest (e.g., Delaware, where EMC might be incorporated). The Perturbation Explanation also mentions the impact on the interpretation and enforcement of the contract, particularly concerning the confidentiality agreement related to a possible acquisition, which may cause undue difficulty for the parties. While the provided scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation suggests a violation if the choice of California law lacks a reasonable basis and contradicts the policies of a state with a greater interest.
